title: QuS2 Skillset
subtitle: Quality management, IT security and data protection Part II
intro: This folder collects practical QuS2 scenarios in the skill trainer format with customer requests, follow-up tasks and auto-evaluable work steps.
The starter case connects root-cause analysis, measurable quality goals, role logic and a resilient kickoff protocol.
Further cases deepen incident scoping, access control, retention, recovery and reliable evidence for security and data protection issues.
The new add-on pools now also cover quality management, data protection, BSI/ISMS and data backup as separate topic blocks.

sections.title: Possible work outputs in the folder

subsections.title: Translate fault patterns into root-cause logic
items: Read complaints, access chaos and missing acceptance as a connected process picture
Separate observation, cause, quality goal and evidence cleanly
Do not confuse symptoms with the actual process root cause
Consider customer view and internal status together
Form a reliable initial picture from scattered hints

subsections.title: Prioritize and justify quality goals
items: Formulate quality goals as verifiable, not just general
Justify the strongest trust risk for the customer clearly
Derive priorities from impact, risk and evidence logic
Link quality goals to roles, process and evidence
Distinguish quick fixes from sustainable goals

subsections.title: Clarify roles and startup responsibility
items: Connect roles with clear startup tasks and responsibilities
Differentiate cleanly between facilitation, service management, technical delivery and quality coordination
Do not let operational pitfalls be handled by the wrong role
Make responsibility and risk visible early in project start
Do not confuse role logic with general team mood

subsections.title: Set up kickoff protocols and evidence cleanly
items: Transfer kickoff results into a binding kickoff protocol
Make roles, open questions, risks and next steps visible
Separate good intent from documented commitment
Read artifacts by impact, responsibility and evidence
Document project start so it can be reliably verified later

subsections.title: Scope incidents cleanly
items: Separate verified facts from open assumptions and hypotheses
Read access path, data scope and recipient group as three distinct risk fields
Trigger immediate measures without a long root-cause analysis
Prioritize the most critical findings in the incident
Do not trade early documentation against speed

subsections.title: Steer reporting paths, roles and evidence
items: Assign communication, technical work, assessment and domain view to the right roles
Secure the best-fitting evidence for each incident aspect
Report verified facts concisely to management or coordination
Document recipient group and data scope separately
Build incident reports so open items remain visible

subsections.title: Secure immediate measures and recurrence prevention
items: Technically contain open link shares, exports and communication paths
Combine data minimization with approval process and logging
Distinguish quick fixes from effective safeguards
Secure recurrence prevention for the same error organizationally and technically
Use evidence not only for clarification but also for prevention

subsections.title: Control approvals and least privilege for remote access
items: Differentiate person-bound, time-limited access from shared permanent access
Link work scope, target system and access breadth cleanly
Set approvals not only formally but also technically effective
Do not confuse trust in partners with missing access control
Read least privilege, shutdown point and logging as one package

subsections.title: Manage maintenance windows and fallback paths
items: Plan time window, net working time and completion checks realistically
Separate responsibilities for access, operations and maintenance cleanly
Do not invent a fallback path only in the error case
Think maintenance only within the approved window and scope
Plan tests and deactivation as part of the window

subsections.title: Secure completion evidence after remote access
items: Combine session log, change findings and deactivation evidence
Distinguish a successful completion from a merely claimed completion
Keep open residual points visible even after a successful test
Document evidence so access can be reconstructed later
Read completion notice, logs and residual-point logic together

subsections.title: Differentiate retention and exceptions cleanly
items: Consider open cases, evidence needs and purely technical working copies separately
Distinguish blanket deletion from data-type-based decisions
Keep exception reasons and follow-ups visible
Do not lump mailbox, cache, export folders and support material together
Do not confuse storage arguments with data protection or evidence logic

subsections.title: Secure deletion decisions and evidence
items: Define standard handling and exception points per data type
Read deletion log, exception list and sample evidence together
Separate documentation duties from pure working copies
Prove the final state technically and organizationally, not just claim it
Recognize shadow inventories as a result of misguided caution

subsections.title: Manage cleanup close to operations
items: Separate roles for domain exceptions, technical execution and documentation
Trigger cleanup only after proper review, not blindly
Plausibly calculate volumes, exceptions and standard cleanup
Link mailboxes, caches and folders with follow-up and completion
Deliver a readable cleanup status for Friday or the next review

subsections.title: Define restore scope and priorities
items: Distinguish green backup status messages from a truly verified restore
Narrow down backup state, test environment and components cleanly
Name domain samples before the test
Prioritize restore scope by system context, not gut feeling
Plan recovery only with a reproducible test state

subsections.title: Manage recovery and dependencies
items: Restore components by dependency, not convenience
Separate roles for tech, domain view, test window and approval cleanly
Plan recovery time and buffer in the test window plausibly
Read logs, samples and restore order as one flow
Keep production and test environments strictly separate

subsections.title: Secure restore evidence and approval
items: Distinguish technical restore from a domain-validated recovery
Write completion findings with residual items and follow-up clearly
Keep document, schedule and access checks in one shared success picture
Build evidence for audit or management so it remains readable later
Do not close a successful test without explicitly named risks

subsections.title: Define access review and scope
items: Keep standard profiles, special rights and legacy accounts in one review view
Define scope cleanly per system, account type and role change
Distinguish list exports from a true needs assessment
Prioritize shared accounts, partner access and old project accounts correctly
Separate calm standard cases from real review risks

subsections.title: Steer role recertification and exceptions
items: Check current role needs against old assignments and substitutions
Keep time-limited exceptions only with reason, end date and responsibility
Interlock domain view, operations and security cleanly in the review
Do not keep special rights running out of habit
Distinguish role confirmation from blanket trust

subsections.title: Secure access removal and evidence
items: Document removal, downgrade and justified retention cleanly
Pull export status, implementation and residual points into one closing view
Handle suspicious accounts and uncritical standard cases differently
Provide audit-ready evidence instead of mere list screenshots
Keep open items visible with follow-up

subsections.title: Prioritize vulnerability situation
items: Read finding, reachability and operational impact together
Do not treat every scan hint with the same urgency
Separate immediately containable risks from planned patch topics
Distinguish finding documentation from mere tool screenshots
Do not confuse time pressure with hectic blind patching

subsections.title: Steer interim measures and patch window
items: Think ACL fix, access removal and prepared patch path as one package
Do not misunderstand interim measures as a replacement for the actual patch
Define test scope, fallback path and responsibilities before the window
Couple technical execution and domain view in the maintenance window
Distinguish purely formal changes from truly controllable measures

subsections.title: Secure retest and completion evidence
items: Keep retest result, implementation evidence and residual points in one closing view
Do not confuse greener scanner results with reliable evidence
Keep open exceptions visible with follow-up
Make a clear closing decision readable per finding
Provide customer, audit and operations with the same factual status

subsections.title: Define provider offboarding and scope
items: Do not confuse contract end with a complete domain offboarding
Collect affected data, portals, mailboxes and technical access cleanly
Make return scope and open exceptions visible early
Distinguish formal contract closure from actual data and access clarification
Do not leave residual inventories with the provider hidden in the process

subsections.title: Steer data return and access removal
items: Think return, transfer and technical removal as one offboarding package
Handle partner portal, API access and support mailbox differently but in alignment
Do not let domain exceptions run instead of a clean removal
Link return protocols with technical removal evidence
Only carry open questions into transition with a deadline and responsibility

subsections.title: Secure deletion confirmation and closure
items: Pull return, deletion and completion evidence together per area
Do not confuse a bulk email with reliable offboarding documentation
Keep exceptions and residual points visible for month-end
Keep technical removals and domain handovers in one closing view
Provide audit, procurement and domain team with the same evidence status

subsections.title: Manage device loss and immediate lock
items: Tie loss report, last sighting and immediate lock together
Do not wait for a possible find before lock and documentation start
Distinguish MDM status from general inventory information
Combine team circumstances and immediate technical measures sensibly
Do not confuse acute locking with later root-cause clarification

subsections.title: Scope sync access and residual data
items: Handle mail sync, calendars, upload cache and tokens separately but aligned
Do not dismiss local residual data out of convenience
Read session or token revocation as part of device loss
Keep technical lock and data assessment in one shared risk picture
Distinguish general app usage from specifically affected data types

subsections.title: Secure MDM evidence and closure
items: Keep MDM status, revoked access and residual data assessment in one closing view
Do not confuse a chat note with reliable incident documentation
Keep open points like device recovery or remote wipe visible with follow-up
Bring management, audit and domain team to the same documented status
Make technical evidence and residual risk readable together

subsections.title: Define alerting and contact status
items: Read contact list, reachability and alert level together
Distinguish formal availability from actually usable contact paths
Make outdated numbers and contact gaps visible early
Do not think internal and external reachability separately
Do not confuse calendar knowledge with verified alert readiness

subsections.title: Steer on-call and escalation
items: Build first contact, backup, fallback and partner contact as an escalation sequence
Do not try to improvise unclear coverage in an emergency
Link internal roles and external hotline logically
Separate technically critical on-call roles from general team availability
Make escalation paths not only documented but verifiable

subsections.title: Secure alerting evidence and closure
items: Keep updated contact status, test results and residual points in one closing view
Do not confuse a list with a truly verified alerting status
Keep unverified partner contacts openly visible
Provide audit, operations and emergency coordination with the same evidence view
Keep residual points clearly tracked for Friday or the next test

subsections.title: QM/QS: Basics, standards and audit
items: Clearly separate QM and QS
Classify standards, guidelines, audit and certification
Understand quality management as the framework for the process
Read workstation protocols and project-accompanying QA as evidence

subsections.title: QM/QS: PDCA and quality planning
items: Plan quality goals, criteria and evidence
Understand quality control in the ongoing process
Apply PDCA as a cycle from planning to adjustment

subsections.title: QM/QS: Workstation protocol and evidence
items: Document the setup of a workstation
Document project-accompanying quality assurance

subsections.title: Information security and data protection
items: Differentiate information security and data protection as foundations
Classify confidentiality, integrity and availability
Observe operational requirements and legal rules

subsections.title: TOM, GDPR and BDSG
items: Recognize technical and organizational measures
Classify GDPR and BDSG as the legal framework
Understand the role of the data protection officer

subsections.title: BSI, ISMS and baseline protection
items: Classify BSI standards and ISO 2700x framework
Distinguish IT baseline protection manual and ISMS
Read IT security management as an organizational task

subsections.title: Protection needs and risk
items: Plan and assess protection needs analysis
Derive risk classification and security requirements

subsections.title: Technical safeguards
items: Classify access controls, encryption, authentication, firewall and hashes

subsections.title: Backup strategies and generation principle
items: Distinguish full, incremental and differential
Classify GFS and generation rotation
Understand offsite backup, retention period and backup window

subsections.title: Backup, restore and evidence
items: Read restore test and backup log as evidence
Combine recovery and documentation cleanly
